description: Diagnostic logic trees for guiding advisor conversations. Each tree maps a client presenting problem to a recommended template pathway. Walk the nodes using the IF conditions and ask the question at each branch point. Terminal nodes (type: 'recommendation') output the templates to suggest.
usage_notes: Use 'quickfire' as the default top-level entry point when the presenting problem is general or vague.
Use 'frameworks_find' as a secondary triage when the client cannot articulate a specific problem type.
Revenue Model is noted as a support tool throughout — choose the appropriate What-if Analysis template based on the client's industry.
Template names map directly to titles in templates.json.
Nodes of type 'assessment' are gate checks — evaluate before asking the initial question.
Nodes of type 'question' require advisor to ask the stated question and branch based on response.
Nodes of type 'recommendation' are terminal — output the listed templates.

trees.name: Quickfire Logic
trees.description: Top-level entry point for general business performance concerns. Splits between survival risk and growth aspiration.

nodes.condition: Client states: 'I'm unhappy about our business performance'
nodes.question: Do you mean you're worried about the business 'going under' or just wish it could do better?
nodes.notes: This establishes the primary divergence. Both branches can be supported with a Revenue Model.
support_templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)

branches.answer_pattern: worried about going under / survival / business failing
wish it could do better / growth / improvement

nodes.condition: Client responds: 'I'm worried we might go under'
nodes.action: Start with the Quick Position model, followed by the Worst Case model.
nodes.notes: Calculate the number of trading cycles to determine if there is time to adapt.

branches.answer_pattern: situation is dire but client is in denial or reluctant to make tough decisions
Quick Position model shows no time — imminent failure
Quick Position model shows at risk but salvage is possible

nodes.condition: Situation is dire BUT client seems to be in denial or struggling to make tough decisions
nodes.action: Take them through the Worst Case (Liquidation) model.
nodes.notes: This provides 'Contrast Perspective' — helps the client see that acting quickly will result in less pain later.

nodes.condition: Quick Position model determines there is 'No time — Imminent Failure'
nodes.action: Direct the client towards the Receivership vs Liquidation video.
nodes.notes: Determine if a Creditor Compromise is possible.

nodes.condition: Quick Position model determines the business is 'At Risk but Salvage Possible'
nodes.action: Complete the Covid Agenda Programme (Feasibility, Scenarios, Contingency, Fundamentals).
nodes.notes: Following this, set KPIs and arrange regular financial performance reviews.

nodes.condition: Client responds: 'I mean I wish it could do better'
nodes.action: Start with the Quickfire Diagnosis Template.
nodes.notes: The template makes problem-solving logic visible to the client, empowering them and teaching them a logic pathway. Focus areas: Get Organised, Get the Job, Do the Job.

branches.answer_pattern: needs foundational improvement / just getting started with advisory
diagnosis needs to continue / ongoing review required

nodes.condition: Proceeding down the 'wish it could do better' pathway
nodes.action: Consider the Lite Fundamentals Programme (in whole or in parts).
nodes.notes: This is the easiest place to start and often provides the best value for money for the client.

nodes.condition: Need to continue the diagnosis on the 'wish it could do better' pathway
nodes.action: Consider using the Pivot template.
nodes.notes: Check progress against Revenue Model variables. Discuss with the team or an Advisor-e coach if required.
support_templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)

trees.name: Client Sales Logic
trees.description: Diagnoses sales problems by first splitting between volume of sales and profitability, then drilling into the specific cause.

nodes.condition: Client states: 'I'm not making enough Sales'
nodes.question: Do you mean the number of Sales or do you mean the Profit left over afterwards?
nodes.notes: Both branches can be supported with a Revenue Model.
support_templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)

branches.answer_pattern: number of sales / volume / not enough customers
profit / margin / money left over

nodes.condition: Client responds: 'I mean the number of sales'
nodes.action: Start with the Customer Journey to determine if the issue is Marketing or Selling.
nodes.notes: Support with a Revenue Model. Covid Agendas combines the Customer Journey with Lite Fundamentals Marketing & Selling for a comprehensive review.
support_templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.question: Looking at your Customer Journey — where does the breakdown seem to be: attracting people to you (Marketing) or converting them once they're in front of you (Selling)?

branches.answer_pattern: marketing / attracting customers / getting found / awareness
selling / conversion / closing / presenting value
beyond fundamentals / advanced / already doing the basics

nodes.condition: Issue is identified as Marketing
nodes.action: Proceed to Lite Fundamentals Marketing.
nodes.notes: Ascertain if the challenge is knowing WHO to target, or knowing WHAT to say and which channel to say it on.

nodes.condition: Issue is identified as Selling
nodes.action: Proceed to Lite Fundamentals Selling.
nodes.notes: Focuses strictly on sales fundamentals.

nodes.condition: Moving beyond fundamentals into advanced strategies
nodes.action: Conduct an Advanced Sales & Marketing Review.
nodes.notes: Involves reviewing AIDCRA, Branding Digital Funnel & Storyboard Pages, alongside Pricing, Packaging, Bundling & Distribution Pages.

nodes.condition: Client responds: 'I mean the profit left over'
nodes.action: Start with the 8 Profit Levers or Profit Levers & Blue Ocean.
nodes.notes: Use to determine if the issue relates to Variable, Activity or Fixed Expenses. 'Profit Levers & Blue Ocean' is suited for more advanced advisors and discerning clients.
support_templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.question: Is the profit issue mainly coming from your variable costs and activity, or are you not sure where the leak is?

branches.answer_pattern: variable activity / cost of sales / variable expenses
not sure / don't know / unclear about numbers

nodes.condition: Profit issue involves Variable Activity
nodes.action: Focus the review on variable activities.
nodes.notes: Maps toward the advanced pricing and packaging review pages.

nodes.condition: Client is 'Not Sure?' about their profit levers or expense types
nodes.action: Acknowledge a 'Rubbish in Rubbish Out' scenario.
nodes.notes: Clarification of business data and inputs is likely needed before applying specific models.

trees.name: Cashflow Logic
trees.description: Diagnoses cash management problems by splitting between slow cash arrival and a lack of financial clarity.
entry_triggers: don't know where the money goes

nodes.condition: Client states: 'I think I have a Cash Management problem'
nodes.question: Do you mean the money is slow to arrive, or you don't know where you stand?
nodes.notes: This establishes whether the issue is timing of cash flow vs operating margin and fixed costs.

branches.answer_pattern: money is slow / timing / cash comes in eventually but disappears / no reserve
don't know where I stand / no visibility / unclear on finances

nodes.condition: Client responds: 'The money is slow to arrive — I catch up eventually but then it's gone again. I never have a safe reserve.'
nodes.action: Start with the Working Capital Cycle and support with a Revenue Model.
nodes.notes: The Working Capital Cycle helps layer the message for clients. For newer owners it explains money movement. For advanced owners it leads to just-in-time stock control, lean systems, etc.
support_templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.question: Is the root cause that you simply don't have enough capital, or is it more about how you're managing the cash you do have?

branches.answer_pattern: lack of capital / not enough money to begin with / funding gap
poor cash management / timing / debtors / spending habits

nodes.condition: Root cause identified as lack of Capital
nodes.action: Utilise the Loan Estimator model, Personal Budget, and Forecast models.
nodes.notes: Brainstorm as a team to add cash management tactics to the support page in the Working Capital Cycle template (Firm Manager mode).

nodes.condition: Root cause identified as poor cash management
nodes.action: Brainstorm cash tactics, use Debtor Protocols and Lite Fundamentals Data.
nodes.notes: Brainstorm as a team to add cash management tactics to the support page in the Working Capital Cycle template (Firm Manager mode).

nodes.condition: Client responds: 'I can't say where I stand with complete confidence.'
nodes.action: Start by generating a Financial Dashboard and support with a Revenue Model.
nodes.notes: Generating a dashboard quickly identifies if current accounting systems provide accurate data and if the owner understands performance and activity ratios. This reveals how deep you need to explore their systems, finance team, or owner education.
support_templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.question: Is the issue that your accounting systems aren't giving you reliable data, or is it that there's just no regular process for reviewing the numbers?

branches.answer_pattern: accounting systems are flawed / unreliable data / bad records
no structured review / not reviewing regularly / numbers exist but not used

nodes.condition: Accounting systems/procedures are flawed
nodes.action: Implement Accounting best practice (newer business owners or junior finance teams) or a Financial Systems Review (more complex business cases).
nodes.notes: Can also refer to the Working Capital Cycle explanation with the client if required.

nodes.condition: There's no structured/regular review of financial performance
nodes.action: Initiate Dashboard Discussions and Ratio Analysis.
nodes.notes: Can also refer to the Working Capital Cycle explanation with the client if required.

trees.name: Governance & Leadership Logic
trees.description: Diagnoses governance, leadership, and decision-quality issues. Covers leadership style fit, team functioning levels (the Waterline), boardroom conduct and manipulation tactics, psyche errors, and formal decision-making frameworks. Uses a readiness gate to ensure foundational management is in place before advancing to formal governance tools.

nodes.condition: Client has no clear business objectives, accepts task failure as normal, or lacks honest feedback loops
nodes.action: Determine that management is ineffective — it is Too Early For Governance.
nodes.notes: A facade of governance will not act as a shield. If objectives lack priority or measurement, or the culture hides mistakes rather than learning from them, foundational management must be fixed first. Do not advance to formal governance tools until this gate is passed.
nodes.gate_question: Before diving into governance: does the business have clear, measurable objectives? Do they track task outcomes? Is there a culture of honest feedback where mistakes are shared and learned from?

nodes.condition: Client states they have governance or leadership issues
nodes.question: Is the primary concern about how your leadership style and culture are set up, how the team functions and behaves together, or how decisions are made and governed?
nodes.notes: This three-way split covers the three distinct dimensions of governance: (1) alignment of leadership style to business strategy, (2) team conduct and dynamics, and (3) the quality and formality of decision-making processes.

branches.answer_pattern: leadership style / culture / how we lead / leadership fit / leadership alignment / style wrong
team functioning / team behaviour / team dynamics / team not working / conduct / board conduct / boardroom / how the team works
decision making / decision quality / accountability / rules / protocols / adherence / structures / governance process

nodes.condition: Primary concern is leadership style or culture alignment
nodes.action: Explore the fit between leadership style and business strategy.
nodes.notes: It is a critical error to adopt a leadership style based on personal preference or by mimicking an aspirational figure. The style must match the business strategy: a Creativity Culture (open-air debate, flat structures, flexible environment, individual incentives for new ideas) or a Discipline Culture (strict best-practice rules, fixed structures, defined reporting lines, team-based performance). Heavily compliant or safety-driven industries almost always require Discipline — applying a creative culture in those contexts is a dangerous misalignment.
nodes.question: Is the issue that the current leadership style feels misaligned with where the business is heading, or that team culture has drifted away from what was originally intended?

branches.answer_pattern: leadership misaligned / wrong style / doesn't fit strategy / not the right approach / needs to change
culture drifted / culture wrong / team culture changed / not what we wanted / environment has changed

nodes.condition: Leadership style does not match the business's strategic needs
nodes.action: Conduct a Leadership Review to formally audit the fit between leadership style and strategy.
nodes.notes: Use the Creativity vs Discipline framework: if the strategy requires innovation, leadership must encourage open debate, flat structures, and reward individual ideas. If the strategy requires safety, precision and consistency, leadership must be built around discipline, defined reporting lines and team performance. Document the pros and cons of each approach for the specific industry. Ensure that recruitment, meeting structures, and conflict resolution genuinely reflect the strategy — not just the leader's preferences.

nodes.condition: Team culture has drifted from the intended environment
nodes.action: Use the Organisational Review to diagnose the gap between intended and current culture.
nodes.notes: Culture drift often produces teams stuck at Level 2 or Level 3 of the Team Functioning Waterline — Role Observance (conduct dominated by the top dog, unspoken hesitancy) or Conformist Contribution (members drive within their lanes, contribute for self-promotion over team benefit). The Organisational Review creates a formal baseline. Board Member Conduct provides the framework for re-establishing standards. The Leadership Review ensures the leader's style is actively rebuilding the right environment.

nodes.condition: Primary concern is how the team or board functions and behaves
nodes.action: Assess the team's Waterline — the level at which they operate most often.
nodes.notes: The 5 Levels of Team Functioning (Waterline): Level 1 — Flow State (authentic expression, creative abrasion, individual accountability, diversity of insight valued over virtue signalling). Level 2 — Role Observance (title-based respect, conduct dominated by the top dog, unspoken hesitancy). Level 3 — Conformist Contribution (average performance, structure and titles defend opinions, members drive within their lanes). Level 4 — Machiavellian (duplicitous, divisional rivalry, ideas undermined on purpose). Level 5 — Violation (survival mode, immoral acts ignored, theft or absenteeism seen as payback for poor management). Step 1 in any intervention: have each member independently record the level they believe the board operates at most often, then discuss as a group.
nodes.question: Would you say the team is broadly collaborative but lacks structure or confidence, or is there active tension, political behaviour, or manipulation affecting how the team operates?

branches.answer_pattern: broadly collaborative / gets along / needs structure / lacks confidence / something missing / hesitant / functions but could be better
active tension / politics / manipulation / undermining / divisive / power plays / people not following conduct / dysfunction

nodes.condition: Team functions at Level 1-2 — collaborative but lacking formal structure or habits
nodes.action: Introduce Governance and establish an Annual Board Plan to create consistent meeting rhythms and conduct standards.
nodes.notes: Level 1-2 teams need a formal Code of Conduct and agreed meeting protocols before dysfunctions develop. The Code of Conduct should require: Align (act in the company's best interests, commit to common goals), Respect (take personal responsibility, participate in robust debate, concede defeat with grace, immediately disclose conflicts of interest), and Structure (contribute to strategy, embrace collaboration frameworks, abide by disciplinary procedures). A key practical step: agree on a team safe word — a phrase such as 'play the ball not the player' — that any member can invoke during a meeting to pause and redirect conduct without making it personal.

nodes.condition: Team is operating at Level 3-4 — political or manipulative behaviour is active
nodes.action: Use Board Member Conduct to identify and name the manipulation tactics in play.
nodes.notes: Common boardroom manipulation tactics: The Power Broker (pulls rank regardless of evidence or prudence), The Assassin (fuels divisive personality alliances and an us-against-them culture), The Fence Mender (abstains from authentic contribution and goes along to get along), The Taxi Driver (blindly steers discussion in any direction to frustrate the room), Cold Logic (uses rigid logic to attack the person rather than the issue). Naming these openly — through the Code of Conduct — neutralises the behaviour without personalising the confrontation.
nodes.question: Is the dysfunction showing up mainly as dominant individuals shutting things down, divisive alliances forming between members, or people disengaging and contributing nothing meaningful?

branches.answer_pattern: dominant individuals / pulling rank / shutting down / power broker / assassin / divisive alliances / undermining
disengaging / not contributing / going along / fence mender / passive / no accountability / silent

nodes.condition: Dysfunction driven by dominant or divisive individuals (Power Broker, Assassin)
nodes.action: Focus on Board Member Conduct, Code of Conduct, and Quality Decisions to redirect authority from rank to evidence.
nodes.notes: Power Broker and Assassin tactics lose their force when authority is redirected from seniority to the quality of the argument. Quality Decisions frameworks (6 Hats, 5 Whys, Draft White Papers) force structured reasoning independent of title. Partner Accountability establishes conflict of interest disclosure and individual accountability so dominant individuals cannot operate unchallenged.

nodes.condition: Dysfunction is passive — fence mending, disengagement, absence of accountability
nodes.action: Use Governance Introduction to re-establish shared expectations, supported by Partner Accountability.
nodes.notes: A team that never argues is not necessarily a high-performing Level 1 Flow State team — it may be a Level 3 Conformist environment where members go along to get along. High-performing teams practice Creative Abrasion: they actively clash ideas, play the ball not the player, and forgive flare-ups quickly because they value the best outcome over false harmony. Partner Accountability establishes individual responsibility and a safe structure for authentic contribution.

nodes.condition: Primary concern is the quality of decisions or the governance of the decision-making process
nodes.question: Is the concern more about the quality of thinking and reasoning behind decisions — biases, blind spots, assumptions — or about having the right formal structures, protocols, and accountability in place?
nodes.notes: Decision quality can be eroded from two directions: internal psyche errors (Confirmation Bias, Complacency, Optimism Bias, Familiarity Principle, Role-Identification) or the absence of formal structures that enforce rigour. Leaders must also distinguish Causation from Correlation and Coincidence, and avoid Deming's Tampering trap of over-reacting to common low-impact variation rather than identifying genuine special-cause events.

branches.answer_pattern: quality of thinking / biases / blind spots / assumptions / ignoring evidence / confirmation bias / poor reasoning / psyche errors
formal structures / protocols / accountability / rules / adherence / documentation / no process / need structures

nodes.branch_name: Branch 3a: Decision Quality — Thinking and Bias
nodes.condition: Concern is about cognitive bias, blind spots, or poor reasoning behind decisions
nodes.action: Use Quality Decisions and 6 Hats to introduce structured frameworks that actively counteract psyche errors.
nodes.notes: The 5 Common Psyche Errors: Confirmation Bias (seeking data that confirms existing views to avoid cognitive dissonance), Complacency (losing focus on the external environment once security is perceived), Optimism Bias (deleting negative information to protect existing beliefs), Over-Exposure/Familiarity (preferring the familiar — better the devil you know — over what is actually needed), Role-Identification (mimicking how a boss should behave rather than thinking authentically). De Bono's 6 Hats forces six perspectives: White (missing information), Red (intuition/feelings), Black (devil's advocate), Yellow (objective benefits), Green (creative alternatives), Blue (sequencing). Draft White Papers formalise the decision workpaper: defined problem, list of solutions with pros and cons, chosen solution with supporting logic, and crucially the counter-argument that proves risks were genuinely considered.

nodes.condition: Concern is about missing structures, protocols, and formal accountability
nodes.action: Build the governance infrastructure using Annual Board Plan, Partner Accountability, and Draft White Papers.
nodes.notes: Formal governance requires: Protocols (defined procedures that must be followed without exception), Tolerances (permissible limits of variation from the original design), Milestones with Early Failure Indicators so the team knows exactly when a decision is going off track, and Independent Review (the person reviewing project success must not be the person managing its implementation). Partner Accountability establishes conflict of interest disclosure and individual accountability. Annual Board Plan creates the meeting rhythm and agenda discipline. Draft White Papers capture the full decision record including the counter-argument that proves risks were genuinely considered.

trees.name: Client Planning Logic
trees.description: Routes business planning needs based on the maturity of the business and owner's planning experience.
entry_triggers: need to plan for the future

nodes.condition: Client states: 'I need a business plan'
nodes.question: Is this for a Startup, or is it a Well Established Business?
nodes.notes: Both paths can be supported with a Revenue Model.
support_templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)

branches.answer_pattern: startup / new business / just starting / new to planning
established business / been operating for years / existing business

nodes.condition: Client indicates they are a Startup Business or Owner New to Planning
nodes.action: Determine whether they are a pure Startup or just New to Planning.
nodes.notes: The 1 page and 4 page plans are excellent for quick reviews of business issues but are mostly void of educative content.
nodes.question: Are you an entirely new business, or have you been operating for a while but just haven't done structured planning before?

branches.answer_pattern: entirely new / startup / pre-revenue / brand new
operating but new to planning / never done formal planning / want to start planning

nodes.condition: Client is a Startup
nodes.action: Use the 1pg or 4pg Bizz Case, and support with a Revenue Model.
support_templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)

nodes.condition: Client is an owner New to Planning
nodes.action: Use the 1pg or 4pg Bizz Case OR the Lite Strategy (Lite Fundamentals Planning), and support with a Revenue Model.
support_templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.notes: Use the Lite Fundamentals Planning template if you wish to develop ongoing strategic reviews. It provides a basic introduction to planning and features a pre-meeting video to put the client at ease.

nodes.condition: Client indicates it is a Well Established Business
nodes.action: Start with the Planning Outcomes Review to choose the specific focus area, and support with a Revenue Model.
nodes.notes: The Planning Outcomes Review helps you choose between: Business Targets, Strategic Orientation 1&2, Organisational Review, or Sales & Marketing Review.
support_templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.question: Using the Planning Outcomes Review — does the focus land on Business Targets and Strategic Orientation, or on Organisational and Sales & Marketing?

branches.answer_pattern: business targets / strategic orientation / where we want to go / goals
organisational review / sales and marketing review / structure and revenue

nodes.condition: Planning Outcomes Review points to Business Targets or Strategic Orientation 1&2
nodes.action: Utilise Powerful Goal Setting or Profit Levers & Blue Ocean templates.
nodes.notes: Profit Levers & Blue Ocean are short-form templates found in advanced Strategic templates. They help explore strategic issues or position Management Reporting or Governance Services.

nodes.condition: Planning Outcomes Review points to Organisational Review or Sales & Marketing Review
nodes.action: Utilise Alignment Statements or Porter's & Pine templates.
nodes.notes: Porter's & Pine are short-form templates that can be used to explore strategic issues or create demand for structured management review services.

trees.name: Staff Performance Logic
trees.description: Diagnoses staff problems by separating attitude (engagement) from competency (effectiveness), then drilling into root cause.

nodes.condition: Client states: 'My staff are driving me nuts!'
nodes.question: Is it their Engagement (attitude) or their Effectiveness (competency) that's the problem?
nodes.notes: If they say they don't know, start with the People vs Process Template and support it with the Wages Review What-if Model. Consider the Remuneration & Incentives template also.

branches.answer_pattern: engagement / attitude / motivation / don't care / wrong people
effectiveness / competency / skill / capability / can't do the job

nodes.condition: Client responds: 'Their Engagement (attitude) is the problem'
nodes.action: Review the Lite Fundamentals 'PEOPLE' template. If you don't have the right team in the canoe, consider the Hiring Winners template also.
nodes.notes: If engagement is an issue on a wider basis, consider using the Leadership Review to determine if the problem comes from the top. If the attitude issue is related to a single person or a few troublemakers, consider using a formal PIP (Performance Improvement Plan) in accordance with HR laws to record specific behavioural adjustments.

nodes.condition: Client responds: 'Their Effectiveness (competency) is the problem'
nodes.question: Is their Effectiveness a result of poor systems or lacking skill sets?
nodes.notes: This establishes a secondary divergence to pinpoint the exact root cause of the effectiveness issue.

branches.answer_pattern: lacking skill sets / don't know how / need training / inexperienced
poor systems / bad processes / no clear process / systems failing them

nodes.condition: Client responds: 'Lacking Skill sets'
nodes.action: Focus on the Delegation & Feedback protocols in the Lite Fundamentals 'People' template. Consider also the Turnaround Behaviours template.
nodes.notes: If staff give 100% but lack experience, determine if current staff are capable (by temperament and IQ) to reach required levels. If they are, develop learning paths based on OBSERVABLE SKILLS. If not, explore the Hiring Winners template.

nodes.condition: Client responds: 'Poor Systems'
nodes.action: Review the Lite Fundamentals 'Process' template. For a more advanced organisation, explore the Organisational Review template.
nodes.notes: This branch is used when staff effectiveness is hindered by operational processes rather than individual capabilities.

trees.name: Frameworks Find Logic
trees.description: Secondary triage for broad or vague presenting problems. Seven branches cover the most common business challenges where the right framework is unclear. Use this when a client cannot articulate a specific problem or when the Quickfire tree does not produce a clear path.
entry_triggers: don't know what the business will look like
not sure which framework to use
does not know what the business

nodes.condition: Client states: 'I don't know what my business will look like in 5 or 10 years'
nodes.question: Do you mean you're worried about the business facing massive disruption, or do you mean you're unclear what you want?
nodes.notes: This establishes the primary divergence. From here, identify which of the 7 branches best fits the underlying problem.

branches.answer_pattern: unclear on milestones / don't know where the business is heading / no vision
stagnant growth / failing to grow / frustrated / losing the battle
struggling to win new customers / customer acquisition / can't find clients
revenue volatile / feast and famine / inconsistent income / up and down
doesn't engage with financials / no budget / gut feel decisions / ignores numbers
concerned about viability / is the business viable / will it survive
struggling to deliver / can't meet demand / overwhelmed by workload / fulfilment issues

nodes.condition: Client is lacking clarity about business milestones
nodes.action: Use the Growth Fundamentals Framework.
nodes.notes: Quick and simple. Uses contrast to reduce confusion — 'Pick a spot on the curve vs Define your Vision in a sentence'.

nodes.condition: Business is failing to grow and the owner is frustrated, feeling like they're losing the battle
nodes.action: Use the Capacity, Capability, Opportunity template.
nodes.notes: Easy to facilitate, memorable for clients, and opens the discovery pathway.

nodes.condition: Client is struggling to win new customers
nodes.action: Use the Customer Journey template.
nodes.notes: Makes it easy to identify the primary issue and downstream effects.

nodes.condition: Revenue is volatile and the owner feels exhausted bouncing from famine to feast
nodes.action: Use the E.O.Y Meeting and Demings Volatility templates.
nodes.notes: Explains the volatility range, encourages a more balanced understanding of data, and provides 4 remedy options to consider.

nodes.condition: Client doesn't engage with financials, lacks an expense budget, doesn't retain profits, and hires staff or purchases assets by gut-feel
nodes.action: Use the Working Capital Cycle Model.
nodes.notes: Provides a plain-English explanation of 'money in movement' related to their job/trading cycles to show how financial management achieves better outcomes.

nodes.condition: Owner is concerned about overall business viability
nodes.action: Use the Quick Fire Diagnosis template.
nodes.notes: Provides a concise triage-logic to identify the severity of the issue and deliver the relevant action plan.

nodes.condition: Business is struggling to meet demand and deliver products/services in a timely, profitable manner
nodes.action: Use People vs Process, Lite Fundamentals People + Lite Fundamentals Process, or an Organisational Review.
nodes.notes: 'People vs Process' helps diagnose the issues. The Lite templates provide simple remedy options. The Organisational Review offers more in-depth suggestions.

trees.name: Sales Process Decision Tree
trees.description: Guides the advisor to the right sales process and approach method based on client type, sophistication, and relationship warmth. Terminal nodes output a recommended sales process (not a template). After identifying the process, use the approach_guidance table to determine HOW to reach out based on the advisor-client relationship.

approach_guidance.relationship_status: Warm & Receptive — easily passes the 'How was your weekend?' test
approach_guidance.discovery_style: Growth Curve, EOY/Volatility Review, Revenue Model, How You Rate tables, Strategic Planning Template

approach_guidance.method: Pre-Approach Email (Quiz or Video Link) then Phone Call
approach_guidance.relationship_status: Warm but Wary — still guarded around advisory arena
approach_guidance.step_detail: 1. Phone to ask for permission to send quiz or video link. 2. Prime the client via online quiz or video. 3. Phone to ask for the meeting.
approach_guidance.discovery_style: Growth Curve, EOY/Volatility Review, Revenue Model, How You Rate tables

approach_guidance.method: Pre-Approach Email Sequence then Phone Call then Intro Meeting before Discovery Meeting
approach_guidance.relationship_status: Cool but Engages — perhaps multiple decision makers; needs to 'see before they move'
approach_guidance.step_detail: 1. Educate via multiple emails or webinar. 2. Position the meeting during phone call. 3. Run a taster meeting before the Deep Dive Discovery/Alignment meeting.
approach_guidance.discovery_style: Growth Curve, EOY/Volatility Review, Revenue Model

approach_guidance.method: Group Positioning — Seminar to introduce concept
approach_guidance.relationship_status: Cool to new ideas — needs time to reflect
approach_guidance.step_detail: 1. Invite to seminar, conduct the event and follow up to invite to a 2. personal discovery session.
approach_guidance.discovery_style: Growth Curve, EOY/Volatility Review, Revenue Model, How You Rate tables

nodes.condition: Advisor wants to approach a client about advisory services
nodes.question: Is this an existing client, a targeted prospect you've identified, or a referral or walk-in?
nodes.notes: This is the primary split. Each path has different logic for how to engage.

branches.answer_pattern: existing client
targeted prospect
referral / walk-in

nodes.condition: Client is an existing client
nodes.question: Has this client done any project or advisory work with you before — beyond compliance, tax, or basic forecasting?

branches.answer_pattern: yes — has done project or advisory work
no — only compliance, tax, or basic work

nodes.condition: Existing client has done prior advisory work
nodes.question: Are you confident enough to start in a more consultative manner — thinking on your feet, scoping their situation, and drawing the conversation to a conclusion without a script?
nodes.notes: Requires being less structured; the advisor must be able to scope and upsell without a safety net.

branches.answer_pattern: yes — confident, consultative
no — not confident enough yet

nodes.condition: Existing client, prior advisory work, advisor is confident and consultative
nodes.action: Use the Planning Outcomes Review to approach and engage. This allows the advisor to scope the client's situation and select an appropriate planning session as the means to upsell into broader advisory work.
nodes.sales_process: Planning Outcomes Review — consultative scoping and upsell
nodes.notes: Leads directly to engagement. The advisor runs the Planning Outcomes Review as a discovery/alignment meeting to frame the opportunity.

nodes.branch_name: Recommendation: Lite Fundamentals (Existing, Prior Work, Less Confident)
nodes.condition: Existing client, prior advisory work, but advisor not yet confident going consultative
nodes.action: Approach using the Lite Fundamentals (Campaign) sales process — more structured and scripted than a consultative approach. Use this to safely introduce the next level of advisory engagement.
nodes.notes: Safely transitions to engagement without requiring consultative confidence.

nodes.branch_name: Existing Client — Fee Resistance & Financial Sophistication
nodes.condition: Existing client, no prior advisory work
nodes.question: Does the client fit a small business profile where you suspect fee resistance, and they lack a solid understanding of basic financial management principles?

branches.answer_pattern: yes — small, fee resistant, lacks basic financial knowledge
no — not fee resistant or financially more sophisticated

nodes.condition: Existing client, no prior advisory work, small business profile, fee resistance, lacks basic financial knowledge
nodes.action: Approach with an education-based session first. The goal is to build the client's understanding of advisory value before pitching services. After the session, check whether the client enjoyed it and wants more — if yes, re-evaluate their profile and move toward the Lite Fundamentals (Campaign) process. If no, park the process until a client project or external event creates the need.
nodes.sales_process: Education-Based Session — build value first, then evaluate
nodes.notes: Educates the client before pitching. If the client engages and wants more, loop back through profile evaluation.

nodes.condition: Existing client, no prior advisory work, not fee resistant
nodes.question: Is the client smaller in size and growth-focused, but has only ever used you for compliance work, basic forecasting, or tax issues?

branches.answer_pattern: yes — smaller, growth-focused, compliance/tax only
no — larger or more complex needs

nodes.condition: Existing client, smaller, growth-focused, compliance/tax only
nodes.action: Approach and engage using the Lite Fundamentals (Campaign) sales process. This is the right fit for smaller clients who are growth-focused but haven't yet been introduced to advisory services.
nodes.notes: Pre-packaged, structured, 1–2 meetings, $6–12k fee range.

nodes.condition: Existing client, larger or more complex needs
nodes.action: Approach and engage using the Total Needs sales process. This client has the scale and complexity to warrant a more exploratory, consultative engagement.
nodes.notes: Consultative and exploratory, 3–5 meetings, 10+ hours, $30k+ fee range.

nodes.condition: Client is a targeted prospect
nodes.question: Based on your first meeting, how would you describe this client's level of business sophistication — are they already thinking strategically, or are they more operationally focused and less experienced with advisory concepts?
nodes.notes: Initial discovery to determine the appropriate engagement pathway.

branches.answer_pattern: sophisticated / strategic thinker
not sophisticated / more operational / less experienced

nodes.condition: Sophisticated targeted prospect
nodes.question: Do they fit the larger business profile — more complexity, higher revenue, potentially multiple decision makers?

branches.answer_pattern: yes — larger business
no — smaller business

nodes.condition: Sophisticated targeted prospect with larger business profile
nodes.action: Approach and engage using the Total Needs sales process — the most comprehensive pathway, suited for a client with both the sophistication to engage at that level and the scale to warrant it.
nodes.notes: Deploys the most comprehensive sales process.

nodes.condition: Sophisticated targeted prospect but smaller business
nodes.action: Use the Lite Fundamentals sales process, but modify your offer to include shorter or longer session times for certain content to match this client's sophistication level. Don't apply the standard campaign template verbatim — adapt the delivery.
nodes.notes: Leads into the standard Lite Fundamentals engagement but requires custom modifications to session depth.

nodes.branch_name: Targeted Prospect — Not Sophisticated, Business Size
nodes.condition: Not sophisticated targeted prospect
nodes.question: Do they fit the smaller business profile?

branches.answer_pattern: yes — smaller business
no — larger business despite lower sophistication

nodes.branch_name: Recommendation: Lite Fundamentals Campaign (Not Sophisticated + Smaller)
nodes.condition: Not sophisticated targeted prospect with smaller business profile
nodes.action: Approach and engage using the Lite Fundamentals (Campaign) sales process. This is the right fit for a less sophisticated client at the smaller end of the market.
nodes.notes: Deploys the streamlined campaign process.

nodes.condition: Not sophisticated targeted prospect but larger business
nodes.action: Despite lower sophistication, the business complexity warrants the Total Needs sales process. You may need to invest more time in educating the client on the value of advisory as part of the engagement.
nodes.notes: Defaults to the comprehensive sales approach due to business size and complexity.

nodes.condition: Client is a referral or walk-in
nodes.question: Does the client have a specific issue or service interest they've come with, or are they open and general in their interest?

branches.answer_pattern: yes — specific issue or service interest
no — open, general interest, no specific issue

nodes.branch_name: Recommendation: Solve Specific Issue + Seed Wider
nodes.condition: Referral or walk-in with a specific issue
nodes.action: Address the client's immediate issue first — but position wider. Solve this specific problem well, then loop other service offers into the relationship over time. Trust is being built; don't try to sell advisory broadly in the first engagement.
nodes.sales_process: Solve specific issue — seed wider advisory in future
nodes.notes: Addresses the immediate pain point while creating a platform for future advisory work.

nodes.condition: Referral or walk-in with no specific issue
nodes.question: Does the client fit the smaller business profile?

branches.answer_pattern: yes — smaller business
no — larger business

nodes.condition: Referral or walk-in, no specific issue, smaller business
nodes.action: Approach and engage using the Lite Fundamentals (Campaign) sales process.
nodes.notes: Deploys the streamlined campaign process for smaller referrals.

nodes.condition: Referral or walk-in, no specific issue, larger business
nodes.action: Approach and engage using the Total Needs sales process.
nodes.notes: Deploys the comprehensive sales approach for larger referrals.

trees.name: Seminar & Presentation Design and Delivery
trees.description: Sequential coaching guide for designing and delivering a seminar, workshop, or presentation. Unlike other trees, this is not a one-shot recommendation — it is ongoing stage-by-stage coaching. Always ask where the advisor is in the process before starting, and jump to the relevant stage. Use the Powerful Seminars reference content (injected separately) for detailed coaching at each stage.
trees.stage_entry_question: Where are you in the process right now — just starting to design the content, already have content and preparing to deliver, or are you close to the event and working on logistics?

nodes.condition: Advisor is at the beginning of the design phase
nodes.question: Before we build anything — who is your exact target audience, and what are the desired outcomes? Write them down before we go further. This is the most important step and the one most people skip.
nodes.action: Do not move forward until the audience and outcomes are defined in writing. Writing forces clarity. A great design makes delivery easy.
nodes.notes: Objective of any public speaking engagement: to position the speaker favourably so the audience would seek or confirm their decision to engage their services. Once outcomes are written, summarise them into principles, then select the single concept that best describes the content.

nodes.condition: Advisor has defined audience and outcomes and is ready to build content structure
nodes.question: Great — now let's build the content structure. What's the one overarching concept or theme for the whole session? Once you have that, we'll break it into principles and then into detail points.
nodes.action: Guide them through the C.P.D. (Concept–Principle–Detail) hierarchy: 1. One overarching Concept. 2. Break into Principles (3–5). 3. Up to 3 Detail points per Principle. Limit Detail to 3 points to avoid overwhelming the audience.
nodes.notes: Each Principle should explain how it relates to the Concept and why it matters before delivering Detail. End each Principle with 'where to from here' action steps. For full-day events, each module has its own C.P.D. but all concepts fit an overarching theme.

nodes.condition: C.P.D. structure is outlined and advisor is considering how to engage the room
nodes.question: How are you planning to keep the audience engaged? Have you thought about how different people in the room prefer to learn — some need proof and facts, others need time to reflect, some want to understand the logic first, others just want to get stuck in?
nodes.action: Guide them to build in content and activities that speak to all four learning styles: Pragmatists (facts, case studies, testimonials), Reflectors (thinking time, note-taking), Theorists (logic, input, understanding why), Activists (hands-on, energising). Also prompt them to plan 'turn to the person next to you' breaks to maintain energy.
nodes.notes: The brain can only absorb what the backside can endure. Break content into chunks. Use peer interaction to lift room energy when attendees seem flat. Be strong and directive when instructing audience breaks.

nodes.condition: Content structure is finalised and advisor is planning the physical event
nodes.question: Let's build your event timeline — the 5-6 week plan that gets everything done without a last-minute scramble. Do you have a venue confirmed and an invitation list ready?
nodes.action: Walk through the 5-6 week plan: Week 1 confirm venue and MC. Week 2 send hardcopy 'First' invitation, build C.P.D. for all presenters. Week 3 invitations arrive, send 'Second' email invitation, presenters meet to align content. Week 4 first full rehearsal, phone contacts for RSVPs. Week 5 'Can't wait to see you' email, pack handouts and name tags. Week 6 final rehearsal, final reminder text. 30 days after: thank you email.
nodes.notes: Always send TWO invitations — hardcopy first (stands out, creates tangibility), email second. Appoint an MC for housekeeping (agenda, breaks, phones, toilets) — these should NOT be covered by the platform speaker. Always capture attendee details in advance.

nodes.condition: Advisor is thinking about how to present — their style and approach
nodes.question: What is your primary intent for this audience at each stage of your presentation — are you trying to inspire action, impart skills, share information, or solve problems together?
nodes.action: Match delivery style to intent: Motivator (inspire action — use at opening), Coach (impart skills or instruct — use when authority is established), Educator (share information — structured, logical), Collegiate/Facilitator (solve problems together — use at close or for team sessions). You can jump between styles in a single presentation. The sequence is typically: Motivator to connect → Coach to position → Educator to deliver → Coach/Collegiate to close.
nodes.notes: Each style has a different tone, pace, and non-verbal approach. The trick is to be consciously aware of which style you are using and why. Never stay in Educator mode too long — it becomes mono-tone. Always return to Motivator energy at the close.

nodes.condition: Advisor is preparing to rehearse and wants to structure the delivery
nodes.question: Let's map out your rehearsal. There are 8 delivery steps that need to be practised in sequence — starting with your opening. Have you thought through your psychological prep and how you're going to connect with the room from the moment you walk on?
nodes.action: Walk through the 8 delivery steps in order: 1. Psychological Preparation (intent, energy, presence). 2. Connect with the Audience (stance, voice, Universals/Truisms/Calling out the ghosts). 3. Honey & Mumford Relevance Statement (W.I.I.F.M. — speak to all 4 learning styles). 4. Describe the Concept/Theme. 5. Self Introduction (brief, punchy, under 1 minute). 6. Set the Scene (rules of engagement, PARK board). 7. Layout the Agenda. 8. Go to Work (3-way engagement, reveal content, use stories and activities).
nodes.notes: There are no shortcuts. Follow 'get it down then get it good' — perfectionism causes writer's block. The Evolution Process: design first, then rehearsal refines feel and audience connection. Run at least 2-3 full rehearsals before the event.

nodes.condition: There are multiple presenters or guest speakers involved
nodes.question: With multiple presenters, the most important thing is that everyone understands the overarching C.P.D. structure before anyone prepares independently. Do all presenters have a clear single-page showing their Principles and Detail and how it connects to the whole?
nodes.action: Establish a single-page C.P.D. layout for all presenters before anyone goes too far in preparation. First presenter frames the concept and introduces subsequent speakers. Middle presenters link to the first and introduce those following. End presenter summarises core principles (bullet points only) and gives a clear call to action and forward look. Presenters must meet to compare content and test anchors early.
nodes.notes: The link between presenters doesn't need to be direct. Don't get frustrated if topics are varied — a creative bridge always works. Be wary of multiple presenters all playing the 4 Values W.I.I.F.M. back-to-back — it loses impact. If using a client as a guest speaker, brief them thoroughly and manage post-event enquiry through your office.

nodes.condition: Event is 1 week out or day-of and final preparation is needed
nodes.question: Have you tested the light and sound in the venue? Assume nothing works until you've tested it yourself. Have you walked the stage with the mic and had someone listen from the back of the room?
nodes.action: Run through the full technical checklist: Walk the stage with the mic — speak loud and soft as you will on the day. Test for drop-out spots. Check mic type (unidirectional = hold at sternum level, body follows head). Check headset mic positioning and ensure belt-pack antennae hangs upside down. Check overhead lighting — covers the speaker, not the plants. Arrange seating so you can walk between attendees. Brief any sound operator on loud/quiet moments.
nodes.notes: Do not do a cursory 'testing 123' and walk off. Speak the full range as you will in the actual presentation. Always have a back-up mic. Make sure the venue matches the theme and ambition of the event. Handouts should include full contact details for all presenters at front or back. Refreshments help slow the pace and create networking opportunities.

trees.name: Trial Fit Method — Revenue Model Presentation
trees.description: A staged coaching approach for advisors learning how to introduce a revenue model to a client who already has high awareness or motivation. Coaches through 5 stages: framing, overview, interaction, assumptions testing, and homework.
trees.stage_entry_question: Where are you in the Trial Fit process right now — are you about to introduce the model for the first time, already in a session and wanting to know what to do next, or preparing to reconvene after assigning homework?
entry_triggers: get it down then get it good

nodes.condition: Advisor is about to introduce the revenue model to a motivated client for the first time
nodes.question: Before we get into the model itself — have you framed the session for the client yet? The tailored suit metaphor is the critical first move. Have you introduced that idea, or are you still at the pre-meeting stage?
nodes.action: Coach the advisor to open with the tailored suit metaphor: 'Think of this like buying a suit off the rack — it fits reasonably well from the start, but it will need a few fittings before it's perfect.' This sets realistic expectations, creates permission for imperfection, and explains why starting with a baseline saves significant time and cost. Reinforce the 'get it down, then get it good' principle — no hunting for exact data today, best guesses are the starting point.
nodes.notes: Even motivated clients may still experience map shock. Never skip the framing stage, regardless of the client's enthusiasm level. The metaphor is the psychological foundation for everything that follows.

nodes.condition: Framing is done and advisor is ready to show the client the model
nodes.question: Good. Now for the global overview — have you shown the client the big picture of what the model contains? The goal here is speed, not detail. What pages or sections are in the model you're working with?
nodes.action: Coach the advisor to give a quick tour of the model's pages — what each section does (job types, revenue tracking, wage and capacity, materials, etc.) — without lingering on any individual cell or formula. Keep it moving. The purpose is to give the client a mental map so they are not bewildered when you drop into a specific section. Avoid explaining anything in depth here — that is not the goal of this stage.
nodes.notes: Map shock often happens at this stage if the advisor lingers too long. Move through the overview quickly and confidently. The client does not need to understand the detail yet — they just need to know the tool exists and roughly what it contains.

nodes.branch_name: Stage 3 — Getting Them in the Water
nodes.condition: Global overview is complete and it's time to get the client interacting with the model
nodes.question: Now we get them in the water. Have you picked a specific section to drop into — one where you can ask the client for a best guess on a number? What section are you thinking, and what question will you ask them?
nodes.action: Coach the advisor to immediately drop down to a specific, easy-to-interact-with section and ask the client for a best guess: 'How many hours would your team typically spend on X?' or 'What's your rough estimate for Y?' Use outlandish numbers if needed — e.g. $263,000 for a build that should cost $80,000 — to show the model reacts dynamically without pressure to find real data. Each change the client makes builds familiarity and ownership. Never stop to chase exact figures at this stage.
nodes.notes: The outlandish number technique is deliberate — it removes the emotional pressure of accuracy and shows the model working in an obviously conceptual way. The client realises: this is about understanding how it works, not getting it right yet. That realisation is the goal of this stage.

nodes.condition: Conceptual numbers are in and it's time to compare them against actual performance
nodes.question: Now for the revealing part — comparing the guesses against actual financial performance. Have you pulled up the real numbers to put next to the estimates? What gap are you seeing — and what do you think it's telling the client about how they've been making decisions?
nodes.action: Coach the advisor to compare the client's inputted estimates against actual financial performance data. If there is a large gap, frame it constructively: 'The model is showing us something interesting here — the assumptions you've been using for pricing/hiring/quoting may not be reflecting what's actually happening in the numbers.' Use this to run scenarios: what happens if productivity adjusts from 85% to 92%? What charge-out rate is actually needed to break even? Common findings: uncharged overtime, paid wet days not factored into rates, unbillable hours dragging profitability.
nodes.notes: This is the most powerful moment in the session — the 'aha' where the client realises their everyday assumptions are disconnected from reality. Handle it with care — it can feel confronting. Frame as discovery, not criticism.

nodes.condition: Initial session is wrapping up and it's time to set the client up for the next meeting
nodes.question: Last stage — setting them up with homework. Have you given them a clear brief on what to do with the model between sessions? What do you want them to come back with?
nodes.action: Coach the advisor to assign focused homework: ask the client to 'have a bit of a play with the model' and reflect on four things: (1) What data can they actually measure in their business? (2) What data matters most to their decisions? (3) How would knowing specific answers improve their decision-making? (4) Is the data actionable — would they actually bother tracking it? When they reconvene, use their feedback to guide the final tailoring — modify the model to fit their strategic planning needs precisely. The more the client contributes, the more ownership they take.
nodes.notes: Do not do the data work for them. The client finding and inputting their own data is what converts them from a passive observer to an active owner of the model. Multiple sessions are inherent to this method — do not try to complete everything in one meeting.

trees.name: Cautious Reveal Method — Introducing Revenue Models to Resistant Clients
trees.description: A staged coaching approach for advisors learning how to introduce the idea of a revenue model to a client who has low awareness or motivation. Works by establishing the 'why' before showing any complexity, using unrelated industry examples and a deliberate time gap before revealing the client's own model.
trees.stage_entry_question: Where are you in the Cautious Reveal process right now — are you preparing for a meeting with a client who hasn't heard of revenue modelling before, are you in the concept anchoring stage, or have you already introduced the idea and are now ready to show them more?
entry_triggers: where do i start with models

nodes.condition: Advisor is preparing for a meeting with a low-awareness client and wants to warm them up beforehand
nodes.question: Before the meeting — have you considered sending anything to warm the client up to the idea beforehand? What's the client situation — is this a regular compliance review, or do you have a specific reason for wanting to introduce modelling?
nodes.action: Coach the advisor to use the 'Phil's a plumber' video as pre-meeting outreach — send it via email before the meeting to frame the concept of why a business would need a model. To find it in the platform: Get the Job → search 'video' → select Video Hyperlinks. Alternatively, a brief priming email works: 'One thing I'd like to explore with you this time is how some of our clients are making better decisions with a simple modelling tool.' Pre-meeting outreach is optional but highly effective when you know the client has zero awareness.
nodes.notes: If the meeting is already happening now or tomorrow, skip this step and start from Step 2. Pre-meeting outreach only applies when there is lead time before the meeting.

nodes.condition: Advisor is in the meeting and wants to introduce the concept of modelling without opening a spreadsheet
nodes.question: In the meeting — how are you planning to open the revenue model conversation? The key is to establish the 'why' before showing anything. Have you thought about how you'll introduce the overtrading concept and the profit sweet spot?
nodes.action: Coach the advisor to open with a question: 'Have you heard the term overtrading before?' Then use a simple visual aid (whiteboard sketch or printed diagram) to explain the profit sweet spot — the ideal operating level where a business is profitable without excessive risk. Explain overtrading: growing too fast, funding it through debt, taking on costs before guaranteed demand, ending up less profitable than before. Let the client feel the uncertainty: 'How much would be required? What are the chances it works? How many extra customers just to break even?' The goal is for the client to feel the problem, not just hear about it.
nodes.notes: Do not open a spreadsheet at this stage. No models, no numbers from their business. Concepts only. This is the foundational step — if the client does not feel the problem, the rest of the strategy has nothing to build on.

nodes.condition: Client understands the overtrading concept and is ready to see how a model helps
nodes.question: Now you want to show them how a model solves that problem — but using a completely different industry. Have you got an unrelated industry story ready? The cafe owner is a classic one. What industry does your client work in, so I can suggest the best contrast?
nodes.action: Coach the advisor to use a story set in a completely different industry from the client's own. Classic example: a cafe owner who is lean and profitable at current scale, wants to expand — how do they know if it's actually worth it? Keep the client focused on the underlying logic, not on mapping it to their own numbers. Ask: 'Does that scenario make sense? Can you see how that applies to any business trying to grow?' Let them arrive at the connection themselves — do not force it.
nodes.notes: The unrelated industry is deliberate. The moment you use their own industry, the client immediately starts analysing their own business — which hijacks the conceptual conversation and risks premature judgment. Keep it unrelated until after the teaser model stage.

nodes.condition: Client understands the concept through the unrelated story and is ready for a simple demonstration
nodes.question: Time for a small taste of how the model works. What type of business does your client run — product/sales-based, or hours-based (trades, construction, professional services)? That will determine which entry-level model to use.
nodes.action: For product or sales-based businesses: use a simple break-even model with the unrelated industry example. E.g. 'How many extra coffees does the cafe need to sell each day just to cover the cost of the new staff member?' Show the maths simply — one variable, one output. For hours-based businesses: use a simple wages model. Show how quickly profitability disappears when sick leave, downtime, and unbillable hours are factored in. Keep it simple — one meaningful insight only. Watch and listen for buying signals: 'Yes, that's really useful' or 'That's kind of intriguing.' A buying signal means you can move to the complex model flash.
nodes.notes: Less is more at this stage. One clear insight is more powerful than a full demonstration. If the client is not yet engaged after the teaser, go back to the concept anchoring — probe their situation, find the angle that resonates for them specifically.

nodes.condition: Client has given a positive buying signal and is ready to see what a full model looks like
nodes.question: You've got a buying signal. Now the complex model flash — briefly opening a fully built model to show the power, without explaining any detail. Have you got a built example from an unrelated industry ready to show? What does it look like?
nodes.action: Open a fully built complex industry model briefly — still using an unrelated industry. Show how multiple variables work together dynamically: marketing costs, customer types, seasonal patterns, staffing, pricing. Keep it strictly high-level. Suggested framing: 'This is what a fully built version looks like for a hospitality business. You can see how changing one variable — like the number of seatings — flows through everything else.' Then close it. Do not explain individual cells or formulas. The goal is a 'wow' moment of curiosity, not understanding.
nodes.notes: Only move here after a buying signal. Do not flash the complex model out of eagerness if the client is still uncertain. Brevity is intentional — you want curiosity, not overwhelm. Close it before the client has time to start asking granular questions.

nodes.branch_name: Step 6 — Propose Trial Fit and Create a Time Gap
nodes.condition: Client is engaged and it's time to secure agreement and book the next meeting
nodes.question: Last step — securing agreement and creating the time gap. How are you planning to close this conversation and propose the next meeting? Have you thought about how to frame the proposal without opening their model today?
nodes.action: Ask: 'Can you see the value of having something like this built specifically for your business — for decisions like hiring, equipment, or planning your growth?' If they say yes — do NOT open their model in this meeting. Explain: 'What I'd like to do is prepare a version for your business and walk you through it properly in our next session.' Enforce the time gap deliberately — the client needs time to take mental ownership of the idea before seeing their own complex data. The second meeting is run using the Trial Fit method.
nodes.notes: Skipping the time gap and showing the client their own model in the same meeting is one of the most common mistakes advisors make. The gap is not a delay — it is a psychological necessity. Without it, the client has not had time to want the model yet, and map shock risk is high. The Cautious Reveal ends here; the next meeting begins the Trial Fit.

trees.name: End of Year Meeting — Planning and Delivery
trees.description: A staged coaching guide for advisors preparing to run an End of Year (EOY) client meeting. Covers the full process from pre-meeting outreach and client audit, through deck selection, meeting delivery, and action planning. Uses the EOY template suite as the practical toolkit at each stage.
trees.stage_entry_question: Where are you in the EOY meeting process right now — are you still preparing and reaching out to the client, about to walk into the meeting, currently in the session and wanting guidance on what to do next, or wrapping up and thinking about follow-up?

nodes.condition: Advisor is in the preparation phase — reaching out to invite the client to an EOY meeting
nodes.question: Before we get into the meeting itself — have you reached out to the client yet? There is a specific outreach toolkit for EOY that makes a real difference to how engaged the client is when they arrive. Have you used anything like emails, a video link, or a quiz to prime them?
nodes.action: Direct the advisor to the E.O.Y Approach Resources template. Key coaching points: SMS outreach outperforms email for engagement — if they have SMS software, use it. The video and quiz act as a micro-step that qualifies client interest and warms them up to advisory thinking before they sit down. For new prospects, outreach qualifies interest before committing to a full EOY meeting. For existing clients, it signals that this year will be more strategic than a standard compliance review. If the goal is to transition the client into Lite Fundamentals, the EOY outreach sequence is the natural starting point.
nodes.notes: Advisors often skip outreach and go straight to the meeting — this is a missed opportunity. Even a single text message with a quiz link measurably improves client engagement rates. The micro-step is low-effort for the advisor and high-impact for the client's mindset arriving at the meeting.

nodes.condition: Outreach is done and the advisor is preparing for the meeting itself
nodes.question: Good. Now for your pre-meeting preparation — have you worked through the Client Review Sheet for this client? The goal is to walk in knowing more about their business than they expect, so you can ask precise questions rather than general ones. What areas are you most uncertain about going into this meeting?
nodes.action: Direct the advisor to the E.O.Y Client Review Sheet — Input. Coach them to review the client across all eight dimensions: Governance, Ownership, Structure, Management Reporting, Financials, Technology, Taxation, and HR. The advisor should identify three or four specific observations to raise naturally during the meeting — not to share the full checklist with the client, but to use it as preparation intelligence. Flag common blind spots: no succession plan, no buy-sell agreement, not using cloud accounting, wrong entity structure. Each gap is both a risk to raise and a potential advisory service to introduce.
nodes.notes: The Review Sheet doubles as a cross-selling map. Gaps flagged during the pre-meeting audit become natural conversation starters during the session. Advisors who skip this step arrive as accountants; advisors who complete it arrive as strategic partners.

nodes.branch_name: Stage 3 — Deck Selection and Meeting Framing
nodes.condition: Advisor has completed pre-meeting prep and needs to choose the right presentation deck
nodes.question: There are four EOY deck variants — Frame, Standard Agenda, V2, and Rural. Which one are you planning to use, and what do you know about this client that's driving that choice? Tell me a bit about the client — their industry, their main challenges, and how emotionally engaged they typically are in these meetings.
nodes.action: Use the client profile to recommend the right deck. Frame: use as an opener for emotionally-driven clients or first-time advisory meetings — it bridges the Personal Zone to the Business Zone before any numbers appear. Standard Agenda: the default for most business clients — covers accounts review, Growth Curve, Volatility, and Start/Stop/Keep. Best for clients with cash or sales volatility. V2: for clients whose primary challenge is revenue probability — they need to focus on capacity to generate reliable revenue. Rural: for farming or agribusiness clients — uses farmer-specific language and aligns strategic spending to agricultural seasons. The Frame deck can be combined with any other deck as an opening section — they are not mutually exclusive. If in doubt, default to the Standard Agenda.
nodes.notes: Deck selection is not just a logistical choice — it is a psychological one. Using the wrong deck for the client type creates friction early in the meeting. A rural farmer shown a standard urban growth curve feels like the meeting wasn't prepared for them. A highly analytical client shown an emotional framing deck may disengage. Match the tool to the person.

nodes.condition: Advisor is in or about to enter the meeting and needs guidance on delivery
nodes.question: Let's talk through the meeting itself. Have you rehearsed your delivery of the key concepts — the Growth Curve positioning, the Volatility section, and the transition from accounts to advisory? And is there anything specific about this client you're concerned about — their personality, their emotional state, or a difficult topic you need to navigate?
nodes.action: Coach the advisor through the meeting structure: (1) Open in the Personal Zone — ask about lifestyle, goals, family before touching any business topic. This makes the advisory conversation feel invited rather than imposed. (2) Identify early whether the client is macro-vision (conceptual, big-picture) or micro-vision (data-driven, detail-oriented) and adjust language and pacing accordingly. (3) The Growth Curve is the conceptual anchor — position the client on the 9 stages to give context to everything else. (4) Use the Volatility section with the WHAT-HOW framework for cash flow issues. (5) Close the advisory section with Start/Stop/Keep — collaborative and co-created. Direct them to the EOY Scripts Only document for exact wording and rehearsal prompts. For difficult client personalities: Angry Couple — match the energy of the most at-risk person first. Depressed client — manage pace and tone, do not get pulled to their emotional level. Angry/rude — stay high-level, use a By the Way redirect. Dismissive of junior advisor — assert status through tone and posture. Withdrawn — fill the room with physical energy and movement.
nodes.notes: The EOY Scripts Only document is underused. Advisors who rehearse the exact language for introducing the Growth Curve or Volatility Report deliver it with far more confidence and authority than those who improvise. Even experienced advisors benefit from rehearsing new concepts before using them with real clients.

nodes.condition: The meeting is wrapping up and the advisor needs to close with a clear action plan and next steps
nodes.question: Last stage — closing the meeting with a committed action plan. Have you thought about how you're going to document what was agreed and what happens next? And is this client one who could benefit from structured ongoing advisory, or are they still at the single-meeting stage?
nodes.action: Coach the advisor to use the General Agenda template to document meeting outcomes — objectives discussed, items covered, and specific action points with owners and due dates. The General Agenda becomes the accountability tool at the next meeting. Three closing questions to always ask: (1) What is the one thing you are most committed to actioning before we meet again? (2) What support do you need from us to make that happen? (3) When shall we schedule our next session? For clients ready for structured ongoing advisory: introduce the Management Reporting Annual Plan — a 12-month governance and strategic calendar. Frame it as their advisory board calendar — it gives them professional governance structure and creates a clear recurring revenue roadmap for the advisor. The EOY meeting naturally creates momentum for ongoing advisory — the Growth Curve, Volatility, and Start/Stop/Keep framework leaves enough unanswered questions that engaged clients want to continue.
nodes.notes: The biggest mistake at this stage is leaving without a booked next meeting. An EOY meeting with no follow-up date is a one-off — an EOY meeting with a scheduled next session is the start of an advisory relationship. Always book the next meeting before the client leaves the room.

trees.name: Facilitation 101 — Framing, Story, and Relevance
trees.description: A sequential coaching guide for the Facilitation 101 framework — the universal 3-stage entry protocol for introducing any advisory concept or template to a client. Covers the opening frame, building a 3rd person story, and securing self-relevance.
trees.stage_entry_question: Which part of the Facilitation 101 sequence do you want to work on — the opening frame, building your 3rd person story, or knowing when and how to secure relevance with the client?

nodes.condition: Advisor wants to coach the opening frame for introducing any advisory concept
nodes.question: Let's start with the opening frame. Have you used the script before — 'I want to run a concept past you and get your feedback; if you like what you see, we'll explore how the concept relates to your business in more detail. How does that sound?' How comfortable do you feel delivering it word for word?
nodes.notes: Each phrase in the frame performs a specific psychological function. 'Get your feedback' shares power. 'If you like what you see' slips the ego and creates an exit. 'How does that sound?' closes the psychological loop. Do not paraphrase — changing the words changes the outcome.

nodes.condition: Advisor is ready to build and deliver the 3rd person story before applying the concept to the client's business
nodes.question: Now the 3rd person story. Do you have a go-to story you use consistently — a chocolate factory, a computer shop, a cafe owner — or are you still making up a new one each time? And when you tell it, do you play both characters in the conversation or do you just describe it?
nodes.notes: The story must be from a different industry to the client's. Use the same one every time — familiarity builds emotional depth. Characterise both sides of the dialogue (play both roles, like a comedian). Goal: create theatre in their mind, not transfer information.

nodes.condition: Advisor is ready to practice transitioning from the 3rd person story to the client's own business
nodes.question: Last stage — securing relevance. How do you currently know when a client is ready to have the concept applied to their own business? What signals are you watching for before you make that transition?
nodes.notes: Self-relevance signals: 'That's like my business...' or 'I have customers who do exactly that...' — only then transition. Park early contributions (acknowledge and defer) until the concept is fully explained. The concept sticks only when the client understands it, sees how it applies, and knows what action to take — all three in sequence.

trees.name: Revealing the Growth Curve
trees.description: A sequential coaching guide for delivering the Growth Curve reveal to a client. Covers global positioning, persona development, the 3-part story sequence, live drawing technique, and how to close without selling too fast.
entry_triggers: how to present the growth curve
trees.stage_entry_question: Which part of the Growth Curve reveal do you want to work on — the opening positioning and script, building your persona, delivering the story, the drawing technique, or how to close the session?

nodes.condition: Advisor wants to coach the opening script that introduces the Growth Curve before the persona story begins
nodes.question: Let's start with the opening frame. Can you deliver the global positioning script from memory right now — the one that explains what the Growth Curve is and why you use it with clients? Have a go, or tell me where you feel uncertain with it.
nodes.notes: The global positioning is brief — one confident paragraph. Its job is to establish what the framework is and why it matters BEFORE they have to cope. Emphasise BEFORE. The transition line into the persona ('Let's take a look through the eyes of...') signals the gear change into story mode — deliver it with energy.

nodes.condition: Advisor needs to build or refine the 3rd person persona used throughout the Growth Curve delivery
nodes.question: Now the persona. Do you have a character you use consistently — name, business, age, backstory, and a quirky product feature? Tell me what you've got, or let's build one from scratch if you haven't settled on one yet.
nodes.notes: The persona keeps the advisor in 3rd person throughout delivery, preventing the client's neocortex from engaging in self-assessment too early. The quirky product feature (e.g. steak and rhubarb pies, chocolate with rust from the mixer) is deliberately strange — the stranger it is, the more it sticks. Advisor should pick one persona and use it with every client.

nodes.condition: Advisor is ready to practice delivering the story and transitioning through the 3-part sequence
nodes.question: The story itself runs in three parts: explain the concept in 3rd person, gain agreement in principle if needed, then create client relevance. Which of those three transitions do you find hardest — staying in 3rd person throughout, knowing when to check for agreement, or making the move to 'so in YOUR case'?
nodes.notes: Part 2 (gaining agreement in principle) is optional — only use it if the client is skeptical or resistant. Engaged clients show self-relevance naturally through questions and comments. Watch for those signals during Part 1 and park any early contributions until the story is complete. The three relevance questions to close: 'Where do you think you sit on the curve?', 'What makes you say that?', 'Where do you want to be, and by when?'

nodes.condition: Advisor wants to improve their physical delivery of the Growth Curve — live drawing, chunking, and page management
